Review: Power Scheduler (Shen et al., 2024)

Power Scheduler: A Batch Size and Token Number Agnostic Learning Rate Scheduler proposes a learning rate schedule derived from an empirical power-law relationship between the optimal learning rate, batch size, and number of training tokens. The schedule continuously decays the learning rate as \(\eta(n) = \beta \cdot a \cdot n^b\) (where \(n\) is tokens seen so far), eliminating the need to specify total training steps in advance — unlike cosine decay or the stable phase of WSD. Combined with Maximum Update Parameterization (muP), the authors claim a single set of hyperparameters transfers across token counts, batch sizes, model sizes, and architectures (dense and MoE).

We ran a 95-run HPO sweep at 37M scale to test whether the power-law schedule improves over cosine decay in our codebase. It does not — the best power-law configurations match but do not beat the cosine baseline at 2900 steps. The rest of this document reviews the paper’s claims, then presents our replication results.

Paper summary

The power-law relationship

The core empirical finding comes from exhaustive grid searches over three proxy model sizes (12M, 36M, 121M parameters) trained on RedPajama. For each combination of training tokens \(T \in \{2\text{B}, \ldots, 256\text{B}\}\), batch size \(\beta \in \{16, \ldots, 512\}\), and learning rate \(\eta \in \{0.0002, \ldots, 0.0256\}\), they find the loss-minimizing learning rate. The result: \(\eta_\text{opt} = \beta \cdot a \cdot T^b\), with fitted coefficients \(a \approx 4.6\), \(b \approx -0.51\). The exponent being close to \(-0.5\) (inverse square root of tokens) is suggestive but unexplored by the authors.

The schedule

Three phases:

  1. Warmup. Linear ramp to \(\eta_\text{power}(N_\text{warmup})\).
  2. Power phase. \(\eta(n) = \min(\eta_\text{max},\; \beta \cdot a \cdot n^b)\) — the learning rate continuously decays as a function of tokens seen, unlike WSD which holds constant then decays.
  3. Decay phase. Exponential decay to 0 over the final ~10% of training.

The authors use \(a = 4\), \(b = -0.51\), \(\eta_\text{max} = 0.02\) for all experiments. The reduction from the empirically fitted \(a = 4.6\) to the deployed \(a = 4\) is not explained.

Our implementation follows Section 4 directly. The power phase computes \(\eta(n) = \min\!\bigl(\eta_\text{max},\; \beta \cdot a \cdot (\underbrace{n \cdot \beta \cdot L}_{\text{tokens seen}})^{b}\bigr)\) where \(\beta\) is batch size and \(L\) is sequence length. The cooldown uses exponential half-life decay: \(\eta_\text{decay}(n) = 0.5^{(n - S)/T} \cdot \eta_\text{power}(S)\), where \(S\) is the step where cooldown begins and \(T\) is cooldown length (10% of total steps). This means the final learning rate is \(0.5 \times \eta_\text{power}(S)\), not zero — a detail the paper leaves unspecified.

Experimental claims

At 1B scale (1T tokens, controlled comparison), the Power Scheduler achieves 58.2% average zero-shot accuracy vs. 57.7% for cosine and 57.6% for WSD — a 0.5-point improvement. At 3B scale, PowerLM-3B (66.8% average multiple-choice) is competitive with Gemma2-2B (66.7%) but notably weaker on MMLU (49.2% vs. 53.0%).

Critique

No theoretical basis for the power law. The relationship \(\eta_\text{opt} \propto \beta \cdot T^{-0.51}\) is purely empirical. No goodness-of-fit statistics (R², confidence intervals) are reported — validation is visual. The exponent \(\approx -0.5\) hints at a connection to noise-scale or gradient variance arguments, but this is not explored.

Continual learning is claimed but not tested. The paper motivates the schedule partly through continual-learning advantages (no need to prespecify total steps), but presents zero experiments on mid-training resume, continual pretraining, or domain adaptation.

No intermediate checkpoint evaluation. Despite criticizing cosine’s poor intermediate checkpoints, the paper never evaluates “anytime” performance — no plots of downstream accuracy at intermediate points during training.

Unexplained coefficient change. Section 3 derives \(a = 4.6\) empirically; Section 4 deploys \(a = 4\) without justification.

3B baselines are not controlled. The 3B comparisons are against external models (Qwen, Gemma, SmolLM) with different architectures, data mixtures, and training recipes. This makes it impossible to isolate the scheduler’s contribution at the 3B scale.

Missing details. The 3B data mixture is described only as “open-source datasets with permissive licenses.” No compute budget (GPU hours, FLOPs) is reported. No training loss curves comparing schedulers are shown.

muP is load-bearing. The entire framework depends on muP for cross-model-size transfer of the power-law coefficients. Without muP, it is unclear whether the fitted \(a\) and \(b\) would generalize — but the paper does not ablate this.

Our replication

We swept amplitude \(\in [1.0, 5.0]\) (scales peak LR) and power_law_exp \(\in [-0.8, -0.4]\) (\(\alpha\)) over 95 runs at 37M scale on C4, training for 2900 steps with the same model architecture and batch size as our cosine baseline (\(\text{lr}_\text{base} = 0.0073\), cosine_learning_rate_final_fraction = 0.1). Three additional runs at 13M use default power-law hyperparameters.

Code 
import json
import os
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path

import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import plotly.io as pio

pio.renderers.default = "plotly_mimetype+notebook_connected"

EXPERIMENTS_DIR_37M = Path("../clearml_experiments/c4_a100x8x4_37m/power_scheduler")
BASELINE_DIR_37M = Path("../clearml_experiments/c4_a100x8x4_37m/main")
EXPERIMENTS_DIR_13M = Path("../clearml_experiments/c4_a100x8x4_13m/power_scheduler")
BASELINE_DIR_13M = Path("../clearml_experiments/c4_a100x8x4_13m/main")

TRACKED_FIELDS = [
    ("amplitude", "Amplitude"),
    ("power_law_exp", "Power_Law_Exp"),
    ("steps", "Steps"),
    ("learning_rate", "Learning_Rate"),
]

SHARED_DEFAULTS = {
    "steps": 2900,
}


def calculate_ema(data, smoothing=0.97):
    ema = [data[0]]
    for value in data[1:]:
        ema.append(ema[-1] * smoothing + value * (1 - smoothing))
    return ema


def _read_csv(path):
    if not path.exists():
        return None
    df = pd.read_csv(path)
    return df["step"].tolist(), df["value"].tolist()


def _parse_config(metadata):
    hp = metadata.get("hyperparameters", {})
    config = {}
    for key, val in hp.items():
        if not key.startswith("Hydra/"):
            continue
        suffix = key.removeprefix("Hydra/")
        if suffix.startswith("model."):
            config[suffix.removeprefix("model.")] = val
        elif suffix.startswith("training."):
            config[suffix.removeprefix("training.")] = val
        elif suffix.startswith("flat_tokens."):
            config[suffix.removeprefix("flat_tokens.")] = val
    return config


def _load_experiment(exp_dir, metrics_data, config_data, kind="power_scheduler"):
    metadata = json.loads((exp_dir / "metadata.json").read_text())
    if metadata["status"] != "completed":
        return
    task_id = metadata["experiment_id"]
    config_data[task_id] = _parse_config(metadata)
    loss_csv = _read_csv(exp_dir / "metrics" / "loss_loss.csv")
    if loss_csv is None:
        return
    steps, loss = loss_csv
    name = metadata["experiment_name"]
    for prefix in ["c4_a100x8x4_37m_", "c4_a100x8x4_13m_", "c4_sp_37m_", "13m_"]:
        if name.startswith(prefix):
            name = name[len(prefix):]
            break
    metrics_data[task_id] = {
        "name": name,
        "steps": steps,
        "loss": loss,
        "final_loss": calculate_ema(loss)[-1],
        "kind": kind,
    }


def get_metrics_data(experiments_dir, baseline_dir=None):
    metrics_data, config_data = {}, {}
    if experiments_dir.exists():
        for exp_dir in sorted(experiments_dir.iterdir()):
            if exp_dir.is_dir():
                _load_experiment(exp_dir, metrics_data, config_data, kind="power_scheduler")
    if baseline_dir is not None and baseline_dir.exists():
        for exp_dir in sorted(baseline_dir.iterdir()):
            if exp_dir.is_dir():
                _load_experiment(exp_dir, metrics_data, config_data, kind="baseline")
    return metrics_data, config_data


def filter_metrics(metrics_data, config_data=None, kinds=None, has_amplitude=None, steps=None):
    out = {}
    for guid, d in metrics_data.items():
        if kinds is not None and d["kind"] not in kinds:
            continue
        cfg = (config_data or {}).get(guid, {})
        if has_amplitude is True and "amplitude" not in cfg:
            continue
        if has_amplitude is False and "amplitude" in cfg:
            continue
        if steps is not None:
            run_steps = cfg.get("steps", SHARED_DEFAULTS.get("steps"))
            if run_steps is not None and int(run_steps) != steps:
                continue
        out[guid] = d
    return out


def get_top_k_experiments(loss_data, k=None, ema_smoothing=0.97):
    sorted_exps = sorted(loss_data.items(), key=lambda x: calculate_ema(x[1]["loss"], smoothing=ema_smoothing)[-1])
    return dict(sorted_exps[:k] if k else sorted_exps)


def plot_loss_data(loss_data, ema_smoothing=0.97, top_k=None, opacity=0.25, title=None):
    if top_k:
        loss_data = get_top_k_experiments(loss_data, k=top_k, ema_smoothing=ema_smoothing)
    palette = ["#1f77b4", "#ff7f0e", "#2ca02c", "#d62728", "#9467bd", "#8c564b",
               "#e377c2", "#7f7f7f", "#bcbd22", "#17becf"]
    fig = go.Figure()
    for i, (_, data) in enumerate(loss_data.items()):
        steps, loss = data["steps"], data["loss"]
        ema = calculate_ema(loss, smoothing=ema_smoothing)
        color = palette[i % len(palette)]
        fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
            x=steps, y=loss, mode="lines", name=data["name"],
            legendgroup=data["name"], showlegend=False,
            line=dict(color=color, width=1), opacity=opacity, hoverinfo="skip",
        ))
        fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
            x=steps, y=ema, mode="lines", name=data["name"],
            legendgroup=data["name"],
            line=dict(color=color, width=2),
            hovertemplate="%{fullData.name}<br>step=%{x}<br>loss=%{y:.4f}<extra></extra>",
        ))
    fig.update_layout(
        title=title,
        xaxis_title="Steps", yaxis_title="Loss",
        height=600, hovermode="closest",
        legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="top", y=-0.15, xanchor="center", x=0.5),
        margin=dict(l=60, r=20, t=40, b=80),
    )
    fig.update_xaxes(showgrid=True, gridcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0.1)")
    fig.update_yaxes(showgrid=True, gridcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0.1)")
    fig.show()


def get_eval_metrics_table(metrics_data, config_data, top_k=None, extra_fields=None):
    rows = []
    for guid, d in metrics_data.items():
        if d["final_loss"] is None:
            continue
        fields = {}
        cfg = config_data.get(guid, {})
        display_fields = TRACKED_FIELDS if extra_fields is None else TRACKED_FIELDS + extra_fields
        for config_key, display_key in display_fields:
            val = cfg.get(config_key)
            if val is None:
                val = SHARED_DEFAULTS.get(config_key)
            if val is not None:
                try:
                    fields[display_key] = float(val)
                except (ValueError, TypeError):
                    fields[display_key] = val
        name = d["name"]
        rows.append({"Name": name if len(name) < 40 else name[:37] + "...", "Final_Loss": float(d["final_loss"]), **fields})
    df = pd.DataFrame(rows).sort_values("Final_Loss").reset_index(drop=True)
    if top_k:
        df = df.head(top_k)
    return df.style.format(precision=6).set_properties(**{"text-align": "left"})


metrics_37m, config_37m = get_metrics_data(EXPERIMENTS_DIR_37M, BASELINE_DIR_37M)
metrics_13m, config_13m = get_metrics_data(EXPERIMENTS_DIR_13M, BASELINE_DIR_13M)

Best power-law runs vs. cosine baseline (37M)

All 37M experiments train for 2900 steps on C4 with the same model architecture and batch size. The top 10 power-law runs are plotted against the best cosine baseline.

Code 
hpo_runs = filter_metrics(metrics_37m, config_data=config_37m, kinds=("power_scheduler",), has_amplitude=True, steps=2900)
baselines = filter_metrics(metrics_37m, config_data=config_37m, kinds=("baseline",))

combined = {}
combined.update(get_top_k_experiments(hpo_runs, k=10))
combined.update(get_top_k_experiments(baselines, k=1))
plot_loss_data(combined, ema_smoothing=0.97, opacity=0.1)
Code 
get_eval_metrics_table(hpo_runs, config_37m, top_k=10)
  Name Final_Loss Amplitude Power_Law_Exp Steps
0 flat_tokens_amplitude=2.4562938425936... 3.438924 2.456294 -0.600178 2900.000000
1 power_scheduler_steps=2900 3.439180 2.492984 -0.610246 2900.000000
2 flat_tokens_amplitude=2.0182975548420... 3.441275 2.018298 -0.600152 2900.000000
3 flat_tokens_amplitude=3.0_power_law_e... 3.441741 3.000000 -0.625490 2900.000000
4 flat_tokens_amplitude=2.9922115592912... 3.443486 2.992212 -0.638252 2900.000000
5 flat_tokens_amplitude=3.0_power_law_e... 3.443640 3.000000 -0.619865 2900.000000
6 flat_tokens_amplitude=2.0_power_law_e... 3.443690 2.000000 -0.600000 2900.000000
7 flat_tokens_amplitude=3.0_power_law_e... 3.444933 3.000000 -0.600000 2900.000000
8 amplitude=3.0_power_law_exp=-0.6_step... 3.444933 3.000000 -0.600000 2900.000000
9 amplitude=3.0245910498500486_power_la... 3.445200 3.024591 -0.599983 2900.000000

Cosine baselines

The cosine baseline pool contains 23 runs with different learning rate and step count configurations.

get_eval_metrics_table(baselines, config_37m, top_k=10, extra_fields=[("warmup_steps", "Warmup_Steps")])

Sensitivity to amplitude vs. exponent

The scatter plot maps each of the 95 power-law runs to its EMA-smoothed final loss across the amplitude \(\in [1.0, 5.0]\) and power_law_exp \(\in [-0.8, -0.4]\) grid.

Code 
import plotly.express as px
import numpy as np

scatter_rows = []
for guid, d in hpo_runs.items():
    cfg = config_37m.get(guid, {})
    amp = cfg.get("amplitude")
    exp = cfg.get("power_law_exp")
    if amp is not None and exp is not None:
        scatter_rows.append({
            "amplitude": float(amp),
            "power_law_exp": float(exp),
            "final_loss": float(d["final_loss"]),
            "name": d["name"],
        })

scatter_df = pd.DataFrame(scatter_rows)

fig = go.Figure()
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=scatter_df["amplitude"],
    y=scatter_df["power_law_exp"],
    mode="markers",
    marker=dict(
        size=10,
        color=scatter_df["final_loss"],
        colorscale="Viridis_r",
        colorbar=dict(title="Final Loss (EMA)"),
        showscale=True,
        line=dict(width=0.5, color="white"),
    ),
    text=[f"A={r.amplitude:.2f}, exp={r.power_law_exp:.3f}<br>loss={r.final_loss:.4f}" for _, r in scatter_df.iterrows()],
    hovertemplate="%{text}<extra></extra>",
))

fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="Amplitude",
    yaxis_title="Power Law Exponent",
    height=500,
    margin=dict(l=60, r=20, t=20, b=60),
)
fig.update_xaxes(showgrid=True, gridcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0.1)")
fig.update_yaxes(showgrid=True, gridcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0.1)")
fig.show()

The lowest-loss region is concentrated at amplitude \(\approx 3.0\) and power_law_exp \(\approx -0.6\). Amplitude has a stronger effect: runs with amplitude \(> 4.5\) hit the learning rate cap at 0.02 and behave identically regardless of exponent, while runs with amplitude \(< 2.0\) never reach a high enough peak learning rate.

Code 
bins = [(1.0, 2.5, "A < 2.5"), (2.5, 3.5, "2.5 <= A < 3.5"), (3.5, 5.5, "A >= 3.5")]

for lo, hi, label in bins:
    subset = {}
    for guid, d in hpo_runs.items():
        cfg = config_37m.get(guid, {})
        amp = cfg.get("amplitude")
        if amp is not None and lo <= float(amp) < hi:
            subset[guid] = d
    if subset:
        best = get_top_k_experiments(subset, k=3)
        print(f"\n{label}: {len(subset)} runs, best final loss = {min(d['final_loss'] for d in subset.values()):.4f}")

A < 2.5: 16 runs, best final loss = 3.4389

2.5 <= A < 3.5: 44 runs, best final loss = 3.4417

A >= 3.5: 23 runs, best final loss = 3.4549
Code 
bins_exp = [(-0.85, -0.65, "exp < -0.65"), (-0.65, -0.55, "-0.65 <= exp < -0.55"), (-0.55, -0.35, "exp >= -0.55")]

for lo, hi, label in bins_exp:
    subset = {}
    for guid, d in hpo_runs.items():
        cfg = config_37m.get(guid, {})
        exp = cfg.get("power_law_exp")
        if exp is not None and lo <= float(exp) < hi:
            subset[guid] = d
    if subset:
        print(f"{label}: {len(subset)} runs, best final loss = {min(d['final_loss'] for d in subset.values()):.4f}")
exp < -0.65: 17 runs, best final loss = 3.4549
-0.65 <= exp < -0.55: 53 runs, best final loss = 3.4389
exp >= -0.55: 13 runs, best final loss = 3.5616

Extended training (5800 and 11600 steps)

A small number of runs train for 5800 or 11600 steps with the power-law schedule.

Code 
extended_runs = {}
for guid, d in metrics_37m.items():
    cfg = config_37m.get(guid, {})
    s = cfg.get("steps", SHARED_DEFAULTS.get("steps"))
    if s is not None and int(s) > 2900 and d["kind"] == "power_scheduler":
        extended_runs[guid] = d

long_baselines = {}
for guid, d in baselines.items():
    cfg = config_37m.get(guid, {})
    s = cfg.get("steps")
    if s is not None and int(s) > 2900:
        long_baselines[guid] = d

combined_extended = {}
combined_extended.update(extended_runs)
combined_extended.update(long_baselines)

if combined_extended:
    plot_loss_data(combined_extended, ema_smoothing=0.97, opacity=0.1)
    get_eval_metrics_table(combined_extended, config_37m)
else:
    print("No extended runs found.")

Cross-scale comparison (13M)

Three power-law runs at the 13M scale use default hyperparameters (no amplitude/exponent overrides). The 13M baseline uses cosine decay.

Code 
ps_13m = filter_metrics(metrics_13m, kinds=("power_scheduler",))
bl_13m = filter_metrics(metrics_13m, kinds=("baseline",))
combined_13m = {}
combined_13m.update(ps_13m)
combined_13m.update(bl_13m)

if combined_13m:
    plot_loss_data(combined_13m, ema_smoothing=0.97, opacity=0.1)
    get_eval_metrics_table(combined_13m, config_13m)

At 13M parameters, the cosine baseline also outperforms the default power-law configuration.

Assessment

The paper’s core empirical observation — that optimal LR scales as \(\beta \cdot T^{-0.51}\) — is interesting and the near-\(-0.5\) exponent deserves theoretical follow-up. But the practical claims are undersupported:

  • The controlled 1B comparison shows a 0.5-point improvement over cosine, well within noise for zero-shot evals. The 3B comparison is uncontrolled (different models, data, training recipes).
  • The headline advantage — no need to specify total steps — is shared with WSD, which performs comparably in their own Table 5.
  • Our 95-run sweep at 37M finds that the best power-law configuration matches but does not beat cosine, consistent with the paper’s small margins being within noise at our scale.
  • The claimed continual-learning and anytime-checkpoint benefits are never empirically validated.

The Power Scheduler is a reasonable alternative to cosine decay, but the evidence does not support adopting it over WSD or cosine in practice. The main contribution is the scaling law observation itself, not the schedule.

Limitations of our replication

  • All HPO runs train for 2900 steps. The three extended runs (5800, 11600 steps) do not cover the full amplitude/exponent grid.
  • 37M parameters is below the scale where schedule differences typically become decisive.
  • Runs with amplitude \(\geq 4.5\) hit a learning rate cap at 0.02, making the amplitude parameter ineffective above that threshold.
  • The 13M power-law runs use default hyperparameters and do not sweep amplitude or exponent.
  • No eval loss or downstream task measurements beyond training loss are available.
  • The power-law schedule implementation follows the paper’s Section 4 but lives on a separate branch that is not merged into main.
